findings.title: Review workflow directs broad source-code exposure into a shareable report
findings.description: The skill instructs the agent to copy the complete contents of every reviewed source file into a consolidated `review-report.md` and explicitly frames that report as shareable with people who may not have repository access. This creates a data exposure risk because proprietary code, embedded secrets, internal comments, or other sensitive material may be aggregated into a new artifact and distributed beyond the original access boundary.
findings.snippet: "Include the complete file content in the \"Full Source\" block so that all review notes are self-contained and can be shared with team members who don't have repo access." and "Save the complete review report (including all per-file notes with full source) as `review-report.md` in the project root."
findings.remediation: Do not include full file contents by default. Limit output to summaries, excerpts, or line-specific findings; require explicit user confirmation before copying full source into a report; add redaction for secrets and sensitive files; and avoid encouraging sharing with users who lack repository access.

metadata.meta_confidence_reason: The SKILL.md explicitly instructs inclusion of the entire file contents in a report and says the report can be shared with people who do not have repo access. This is a direct and verifiable expansion of code exposure.
metadata.meta_exploitability: Easy to trigger during normal use because it is the default documented workflow and requires no special conditions beyond running a review.
metadata.meta_impact: Can expose proprietary source code, embedded secrets, internal comments, and other sensitive implementation details in a new artifact that may be redistributed.

findings.title: Missing optional allowed-tools declaration reduces tool-use transparency
findings.description: The manifest does not declare `allowed-tools`. While optional, its absence makes it harder to evaluate whether the skill's instructions to inventory files and write `review-report.md` align with intended tool permissions.
findings.remediation: Add an explicit `allowed-tools` list such as `[Read, Glob, Grep, Write]` to document the minimum required permissions and improve reviewability.

metadata.meta_confidence_reason: The manifest indeed omits `allowed-tools`, and that reduces transparency about intended capabilities. However, this is a governance/clarity issue rather than direct tool abuse.
metadata.meta_exploitability: Not directly exploitable; the issue affects reviewability and policy enforcement rather than enabling an attack by itself.
metadata.meta_impact: Can make it harder for users or platforms to evaluate whether the skill's read/write behavior matches expected permissions.

findings.title: Skill does not specify a license
findings.description: Skill manifest does not include a 'license' field. Specifying a license helps users understand usage terms.
findings.remediation: Add 'license' field to SKILL.md frontmatter (e.g., MIT, Apache-2.0)

metadata.meta_confidence_reason: The provided skill context explicitly states that the license is not specified, so the metadata finding is accurate.
metadata.meta_exploitability: Not exploitable as a security issue.
metadata.meta_impact: Primarily legal and governance ambiguity; little to no direct security impact.

scan_metadata.llm_overall_assessment: The skill does not contain direct prompt injection, malicious code, or external exfiltration logic, but it does encourage large-scale replication of repository contents into a new shareable artifact, which poses a meaningful source-code exposure risk. Security posture is moderate risk primarily due to over-sharing of collected data rather than active malicious behavior.
llm_primary_threats: Data exposure through aggregation of full source into shareable reports
Over-collection and unnecessary replication of repository contents
Insufficient manifest transparency due to missing tool declaration

meta_correlations.group_name: Source Code Replication and Sharing Risk
meta_correlations.relationship: This finding captures the core security issue: duplicating full source into a report intended for sharing beyond original repository access controls.
meta_correlations.consolidated_remediation: Change the workflow to store only summaries, issue lists, and small redacted snippets; require explicit approval before including full file contents or sharing review artifacts outside the repository.

meta_correlations.relationship: These findings are related metadata/transparency issues in the manifest, but they do not constitute direct exploitation paths.
meta_correlations.consolidated_remediation: Add explicit manifest metadata such as allowed-tools and license to improve transparency and governance.

meta_recommendations.title: Stop embedding full source code in review artifacts by default
meta_recommendations.fix: Replace the "Full Source" section with a short summary plus line-referenced findings. Set `include_full_source: false` by default, and only allow full-file inclusion behind an explicit user confirmation step for approved internal use cases.

meta_recommendations.title: Restrict sharing guidance to preserve repository access boundaries
meta_recommendations.fix: Revise the sharing instructions to say: "Do not share generated reports outside the repository access boundary unless the content has been reviewed and redacted for secrets, proprietary code, and internal comments."

meta_recommendations.title: Declare allowed tools in the manifest
meta_recommendations.fix: Add an `allowed-tools` field listing only the minimum required capabilities, for example: `allowed-tools: [Read, Write]`.

meta_recommendations.title: Add a license field to the manifest
meta_recommendations.fix: Add a standard SPDX license identifier in the manifest, for example: `license: MIT` or the correct project license.

meta_risk_assessment.summary: The skill primarily poses an information exposure risk by instructing agents to copy full source files into a shareable report, while the other findings are informational metadata issues.
meta_risk_assessment.top_priority: Remove the requirement to embed complete source code in the generated review report and replace it with minimal excerpts or references.
meta_risk_assessment.verdict_reasoning: The instructions explicitly direct broad replication of repository contents into a new shareable artifact for people without repo access, which can expand the exposure boundary of proprietary or sensitive code. There is no active network exfiltration or code execution, so this is not overtly malicious, but it is a real data-handling concern.
